seoTitle: Gowin FPGA Solutions - Industrial Vision & Edge AI | BeiLuo Distributor
seoDescription: Explore Gowin FPGA-based solutions including industrial vision inspection systems and edge AI accelerators. Complete technical specifications and distributor support available.

faqs.question: What types of solutions does Gowin offer for industrial applications?
faqs.answer: Gowin offers comprehensive FPGA-based solutions for industrial applications including machine vision systems for quality inspection, edge AI accelerators for real-time inference, industrial communication gateways, and motor control systems. These solutions leverage Gowin's low-power FPGAs to deliver high performance with deterministic real-time response. The industrial vision solution provides sub-millimeter measurement accuracy and defect detection capabilities, while the edge AI accelerator enables neural network inference at the edge with low latency. Both solutions are designed for reliable 24/7 operation in harsh industrial environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to discuss your specific industrial application requirements and receive tailored solution recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Gowin solution for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Gowin solution involves evaluating your application requirements: For vision applications, consider the Industrial Vision Inspection System which provides real-time image processing, defect detection, and measurement capabilities. For AI workloads, the Edge AI Inference Accelerator offers neural network acceleration with low power consumption. Key factors to consider include processing requirements (logic capacity and DSP resources), interface needs (camera, Ethernet, industrial protocols), power budget, and environmental conditions. Our solutions are built on proven Gowin FPGA platforms and include complete BOM lists, technical specifications, and reference designs to accelerate your development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Share your application requirements with our FAE team for personalized solution selection guidance and technical consultation.

faqs.question: What support is available for implementing Gowin solutions?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo provides comprehensive support for Gowin solution implementation including: Technical consultation to help select the right solution for your application, Reference designs and BOM lists for rapid prototyping, FAE support for design review and optimization, IP cores for standard interfaces and functions, Development kits for algorithm validation, Documentation including user guides and application notes. Our FAE team has extensive experience with Gowin FPGAs and can provide guidance on hardware design, software development, and system integration. We also offer training sessions and workshops to help your engineering team get up to speed quickly.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us early in your project to discuss support options and engage our FAE team for technical guidance.

faqs.question: Can Gowin solutions be customized for specific requirements?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gowin solutions can be customized to meet specific application requirements. The FPGA-based architecture provides flexibility to: Modify image processing algorithms for specific inspection criteria, Adjust AI model architectures for target applications, Customize communication protocols for factory integration, Adapt I/O configurations for different sensors and actuators, Optimize power consumption for battery-powered deployments. Our solutions serve as starting points that can be tailored to your exact needs. The modular design approach allows selective modification of specific functions while maintaining proven components. Contact our FAE team to discuss customization options for your project.
faqs.decisionGuide: Discuss your customization requirements with our FAE team to determine the best approach for your application.

faqs.question: What is the typical development timeline for Gowin solutions?
faqs.answer: Development timelines vary based on solution complexity and customization requirements: Standard solution deployment using reference designs can be completed in 4-8 weeks including hardware adaptation and software integration. Customized solutions with modified algorithms or interfaces typically require 8-16 weeks for development and validation. Complex custom solutions with significant architectural changes may take 16-24 weeks. Timeline phases include: Requirements analysis and solution selection (1-2 weeks), Hardware design and procurement (4-6 weeks), Software development and algorithm implementation (4-8 weeks), System integration and testing (2-4 weeks), Deployment and validation (1-2 weeks). Using our reference designs and IP cores can significantly accelerate development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us early to discuss your timeline requirements and explore options to accelerate development.

solutions.title: Industrial Vision Inspection System
solutions.name: Industrial Vision Inspection System
solutions.description: A complete FPGA-based machine vision solution for industrial quality inspection, defect detection, and measurement applications. This solution leverages Gowin Arora FPGAs for real-time image processing with low latency and high reliability.
solutions.longDescription: The Industrial Vision Inspection System is a comprehensive FPGA-based solution designed for modern manufacturing environments requiring high-speed, accurate quality control. Built on the proven Gowin GW2A-18 Arora FPGA platform, this solution delivers real-time image processing capabilities that enable immediate pass/fail decisions on production lines. The system supports multiple camera interfaces including MIPI CSI-2 and parallel interfaces, allowing integration with a wide range of industrial cameras. Advanced image processing algorithms running in the FPGA fabric provide sub-millimeter measurement accuracy and reliable defect detection across various surface types. The solution includes industrial-grade communication interfaces (Gigabit Ethernet, RS-485, CAN) for seamless integration with factory automation systems. With extended temperature range operation and robust design practices, this vision system ensures reliable 24/7 operation in harsh industrial environments. The modular architecture allows customization for specific inspection requirements while maintaining the core processing capabilities.
benefits: Real-time processing with deterministic sub-10ms latency for immediate quality decisions
Sub-millimeter measurement accuracy exceeds typical industrial requirements
Flexible algorithm implementation allows customization without hardware changes
Cost-effective compared to PC-based vision systems and smart cameras
Easy integration with existing factory automation via standard protocols
features: Real-time image capture and processing up to 1080p60
Multiple camera interface support (MIPI CSI-2, parallel)
Industrial-grade reliability with extended temperature support
Flexible communication interfaces for factory integration

technicalSpecs.Detection Algorithms: Blob analysis, template matching, edge detection

coreAdvantages.title: Real-Time Processing
coreAdvantages.description: FPGA-based parallel processing enables deterministic low-latency image analysis, critical for high-speed production lines requiring immediate pass/fail decisions.

coreAdvantages.title: Flexible Algorithm Implementation
coreAdvantages.description: Reconfigurable logic allows customization of inspection algorithms for specific products without hardware changes, reducing time-to-market for new inspection requirements.

coreAdvantages.title: Cost-Effective Solution
coreAdvantages.description: Gowin FPGAs provide excellent performance-per-watt and performance-per-dollar compared to traditional industrial vision systems, enabling competitive system pricing.

coreAdvantages.title: Industrial Reliability
coreAdvantages.description: Extended temperature range operation and robust design practices ensure reliable 24/7 operation in harsh factory environments.

coreAdvantages.title: Scalable Architecture
coreAdvantages.description: Modular design supports single-camera to multi-camera configurations, allowing systems to scale with production requirements while maintaining consistent performance.

customerCases.challenge: The customer needed to inspect PCB assemblies at high speed (120 boards/minute) for component presence, orientation, and solder joint quality. Their existing PC-based vision system had inconsistent latency and couldn't keep up with production line speeds.
customerCases.solution: We implemented a Gowin GW2A-18 based vision system with dual MIPI camera inputs. The FPGA processes images in real-time using custom algorithms for component detection and solder joint analysis. Results are communicated to the PLC via Modbus TCP.
customerCases.result: Inspection speed increased to 150 boards/minute with <5ms processing latency. Defect detection rate improved to 99.7% while false positives dropped by 80%. System cost was 40% lower than competing solutions.

customerCases.challenge: The customer required 100% surface inspection of polished metal parts for scratches, dents, and contamination. The inspection needed to operate in a harsh factory environment with oil mist and vibration.
customerCases.solution: A ruggedized vision system was developed using the GW2A-18 FPGA with specialized lighting control and high-dynamic-range image processing. The system uses multi-angle illumination to highlight surface defects.
customerCases.result: Successfully detecting defects as small as 50 microns. System operates reliably 24/7 in the harsh environment. ROI achieved in 8 months through reduced customer returns and warranty claims.

faeInsights.content: Industrial vision systems demand a careful balance between processing performance, latency, and cost. In my experience implementing dozens of vision systems, Gowin Arora FPGAs hit the sweet spot for mid-range applications. The key to success is optimizing the image processing pipeline - don't just port PC algorithms to FPGA. Instead, design algorithms that leverage FPGA parallelism. For example, we implemented a custom convolution engine that processes 1080p video at 60fps while using only 30% of the GW2A-18's resources. Another critical factor is lighting - spend time characterizing your illumination setup before writing a single line of code. The FPGA can compensate for some lighting variations, but good lighting design reduces algorithm complexity significantly. I always recommend starting with a proof-of-concept using the Tang Nano 9K board to validate your camera and basic processing pipeline before committing to custom hardware.
faeInsights.logic: The decision framework for industrial vision systems involves evaluating multiple factors in sequence. First, define your inspection requirements including resolution, speed, and defect types. Then select camera and optics based on field of view and resolution needs. Next, estimate processing requirements to determine whether you need simple detection or complex measurement capabilities. Based on these requirements, choose the appropriate FPGA with sufficient logic and memory resources. Design your lighting system for consistent image quality before developing algorithms. Develop and validate algorithms on a development board before moving to production hardware design. Finally, ensure your design includes appropriate industrial interfaces for factory integration.

steps: Define inspection requirements - resolution, speed, defect types
Select camera and optics based on field of view needs
Estimate processing requirements - detection vs measurement
Choose FPGA based on logic and memory requirements
Design lighting for consistent image quality
Develop algorithms on development board
Design production hardware with industrial interfaces
decisionFramework.decisionTree: If inspection speed > 100 parts/minute, choose FPGA over smart camera. If measurement accuracy < 0.1mm required, verify optical system before FPGA selection. If multiple cameras needed (>2), select GW2A-55 over GW2A-18.

keyTakeaways: Start with lighting design - it's more important than processing power
Use Tang Nano 9K for algorithm proof-of-concept before custom hardware
Optimize algorithms for FPGA parallelism rather than porting PC code
Consider industrial interfaces (Ethernet, RS-485) early in design
Plan for calibration and maintenance in your software architecture
commonPitfalls: Underestimating lighting requirements leads to poor image quality
Over-specifying FPGA capacity increases cost unnecessarily
Neglecting thermal design causes reliability issues in 24/7 operation
Inadequate vibration protection for cameras in industrial environments
bestPractices: Use IP cores for standard interfaces (MIPI, Ethernet) to reduce risk
Implement self-test and diagnostics for field troubleshooting
Design modular processing pipelines for algorithm updates
Include sufficient margin in FPGA resource utilization

faqs.question: What camera sensors are compatible with this vision system?
faqs.answer: The vision system supports a wide range of camera sensors through its flexible MIPI CSI-2 interface. Compatible sensors include: Omnivision OV5640 (5MP), OV9281 (1MP global shutter), Sony IMX290/IMX327 (starlight cameras for low-light applications), and ON Semiconductor AR series. The system can accommodate sensors with 1-4 MIPI lanes and data rates up to 1.5 Gbps per lane. For high-speed applications, global shutter sensors are recommended to avoid motion blur. When selecting a sensor, consider resolution requirements, frame rate, light sensitivity, and cost. Our FAE team can help evaluate specific sensors for your application and provide interface timing analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team with your specific camera requirements for sensor recommendations and compatibility verification. We can provide reference designs for popular sensor options.

faqs.question: How accurate is the measurement capability of this system?
faqs.answer: Measurement accuracy depends on multiple factors including camera resolution, optics, lighting, and calibration. With a 5MP camera (2592x1944) and appropriate optics, the system can achieve sub-pixel measurement accuracy of ±0.05mm over a 100mm field of view. For higher precision requirements, higher resolution cameras or telecentric lenses can be used. The FPGA-based processing enables real-time sub-pixel edge detection algorithms that improve accuracy beyond the native pixel resolution. Calibration using precision gauge blocks is essential for achieving specified accuracy. The system includes calibration routines and can compensate for lens distortion and perspective effects. For critical measurements, we recommend conducting a gauge R&R study to validate system capability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Define your measurement accuracy requirements and field of view. Contact us for optical design consultation and accuracy validation procedures.

faqs.question: Can this system integrate with existing factory automation equipment?
faqs.answer: Yes, the vision system is designed for seamless factory integration with multiple industrial communication options. Standard interfaces include: Gigabit Ethernet with Modbus TCP/IP for PLC communication, RS-485 for legacy equipment, and discrete I/O for trigger and pass/fail signals. The system supports common industrial protocols and can be configured to match your existing infrastructure. For MES integration, inspection results and images can be transmitted via Ethernet to factory databases. The FPGA implementation ensures deterministic response times for real-time control applications. We provide configuration tools and documentation for common PLCs including Siemens, Allen-Bradley, and Mitsubishi. Custom protocol implementations are possible using the FPGA's programmable logic.
faqs.decisionGuide: Document your existing automation architecture and communication requirements. Contact us for integration planning and protocol configuration support.

faqs.question: What is the typical development timeline for a custom vision application?
faqs.answer: Development timeline varies based on application complexity, but typical phases include: 1) Requirements analysis and feasibility study (1-2 weeks) - define inspection criteria and validate technical approach. 2) Proof of concept (2-4 weeks) - develop algorithms on Tang Nano 9K development board. 3) Hardware design (4-6 weeks) - design custom PCB with appropriate interfaces. 4) Software development (4-8 weeks) - implement and optimize processing algorithms. 5) System integration and testing (2-4 weeks) - integrate with production line and validate performance. 6) Deployment and training (1-2 weeks) - install system and train operators. Total timeline typically ranges from 3-6 months for a complete custom solution. Using standard hardware platforms and IP cores can reduce this timeline significantly. We offer development services to accelerate your project.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us early in your project to discuss requirements and development approach. We can provide phased development options to reduce time-to-market.

faqs.question: How does this FPGA solution compare to smart cameras or PC-based vision systems?
faqs.answer: FPGA-based vision systems offer unique advantages compared to alternatives: Compared to smart cameras, FPGAs provide greater processing flexibility and can handle multiple cameras or complex algorithms that exceed smart camera capabilities. FPGA systems also offer lower per-channel cost for multi-camera applications. Compared to PC-based systems, FPGAs provide deterministic real-time performance with consistent latency, better reliability (no OS to crash), lower power consumption, and smaller form factor. However, PC systems may be preferable for very complex algorithms that are easier to implement in software. The Gowin FPGA solution specifically offers excellent cost-performance ratio, making it competitive with both smart cameras and PC systems while providing the benefits of both approaches. For applications requiring real-time response, multiple cameras, or custom algorithms, FPGA is often the optimal choice.
faqs.decisionGuide: Evaluate your requirements for latency determinism, processing complexity, camera count, and cost. Contact us for a detailed comparison based on your specific application.

solutions.title: Edge AI Inference Accelerator
solutions.name: Edge AI Inference Accelerator
solutions.description: A compact, low-power AI acceleration solution for edge devices requiring real-time neural network inference. This solution leverages Gowin Arora FPGAs with dedicated DSP blocks to accelerate convolutional neural networks for image classification, object detection, and other AI workloads at the edge.
solutions.longDescription: The Edge AI Inference Accelerator is a compact, energy-efficient solution designed to bring artificial intelligence capabilities to edge devices without relying on cloud connectivity. Built on the powerful Gowin GW2A-55 Arora FPGA platform with 128 DSP blocks, this accelerator delivers up to 100 GOPS of inference performance while consuming only 2-5W of power. The solution supports popular CNN architectures including MobileNet, ResNet-18, and custom models, enabling a wide range of AI applications from image classification to object detection. INT8 quantization optimizes resource utilization while maintaining accuracy. With flexible input interfaces including MIPI CSI-2 for cameras, USB 2.0 for connectivity, and Gigabit Ethernet for network integration, the accelerator adapts to various deployment scenarios. The modular design supports field model updates, allowing AI models to evolve without hardware changes. This makes it ideal for smart cameras, industrial predictive maintenance, autonomous robotics, and smart retail applications where low latency and data privacy are critical.
benefits: Real-time AI inference at the edge with 10-50ms latency eliminates cloud dependency
Low power consumption (2-5W) enables battery-powered and thermally-constrained deployments
Support for popular CNN architectures provides flexibility for diverse AI applications
Field-upgradable models allow continuous improvement without hardware replacement
Deterministic performance ensures consistent response times for safety-critical applications
Cost-effective alternative to GPU-based solutions for small-batch inference workloads
features: Real-time neural network inference at the edge
Support for popular CNN architectures (MobileNet, ResNet, custom)
INT8 quantization for efficient resource utilization
Low power consumption suitable for battery-powered devices
Multiple sensor input support (camera, radar, lidar)

coreAdvantages.title: High Inference Performance
coreAdvantages.description: Dedicated DSP blocks and parallel architecture enable efficient neural network acceleration, delivering 10-100x speedup over CPU-only implementations for CNN workloads.

coreAdvantages.title: Low Power Edge Deployment
coreAdvantages.description: Optimized FPGA implementation achieves high performance with only 2-5W power consumption, enabling battery-powered and thermally-constrained edge deployments.

coreAdvantages.title: Flexible Model Support
coreAdvantages.description: Reconfigurable architecture supports multiple neural network architectures and allows field updates to models without hardware changes.

coreAdvantages.title: Deterministic Latency
coreAdvantages.description: FPGA implementation provides consistent inference latency critical for real-time control and safety applications.

coreAdvantages.title: Privacy and Security
coreAdvantages.description: On-device processing keeps sensitive data local, eliminating privacy concerns and reducing attack surface compared to cloud-based AI solutions.

bomList.description: DDR3 SDRAM - Model and data storage

customerCases.challenge: The customer needed to add AI-based person detection to their existing camera lineup without replacing the entire system. Cloud-based AI had too much latency and raised privacy concerns. They needed a compact, low-power solution that could be retrofitted to existing cameras.
customerCases.solution: We developed a compact AI accelerator module using the GW2A-55 FPGA that connects inline with existing cameras. The module runs a quantized MobileNet model for person detection and sends alerts via the camera's existing communication interface. The 2W power consumption allows operation from the camera's existing power supply.
customerCases.result: Detection accuracy of 95% with false positive rate below 2%. Latency reduced from 500ms (cloud) to 50ms (edge). System cost per camera reduced by 60% compared to replacing with AI cameras. Privacy compliance achieved by processing locally.

customerCases.challenge: The customer wanted to add vibration analysis and anomaly detection to their industrial equipment for predictive maintenance. The solution needed to operate in harsh industrial environments and integrate with their existing control systems.
customerCases.solution: An edge AI module was developed using the GW2A-18 FPGA to process vibration sensor data through a neural network trained on normal and fault conditions. The system learns equipment signatures and detects anomalies indicating potential failures.
customerCases.result: Successfully detected bearing failures 2-3 weeks before they would cause equipment downtime. False alarm rate less than 1%. ROI achieved in 6 months through prevented downtime. The modular design allowed retrofit to existing installed equipment.

faeInsights.content: Edge AI is transforming how we deploy intelligence in embedded systems, but success requires careful optimization. In my experience with dozens of edge AI projects, the key is matching the neural network architecture to the FPGA resources. Don't just take a model trained for cloud and try to squeeze it into an FPGA - instead, design or adapt models specifically for edge deployment. Quantization is essential - we typically use INT8 or even INT4 precision to maximize performance. The Gowin GW2A-55 with its 128 DSP blocks can achieve impressive inference speeds when properly utilized. Another critical factor is memory bandwidth - the neural network weights often need to be streamed from external DDR, so efficient memory access patterns are crucial. I recommend starting with a reference design and benchmarking your specific model before committing to hardware. The flexibility of FPGAs allows you to optimize the hardware specifically for your model architecture.
faeInsights.logic: The edge AI development process follows a systematic approach. First, define your application requirements including target accuracy, acceptable latency, and power budget constraints. Next, select or design an appropriate neural network architecture that balances accuracy with computational requirements. Then train your model and quantize it to INT8 precision for efficient FPGA implementation. Estimate the FPGA resource requirements including DSP blocks, memory, and logic elements. Based on these estimates, choose the appropriate Gowin FPGA that meets your needs. Implement the inference engine optimized for your target model architecture. Validate both accuracy and performance on actual hardware. Finally, optimize the implementation based on profiling results to meet your targets.

steps: Define application requirements - accuracy, latency, power budget
Select or design appropriate neural network architecture
Train and quantize model for INT8 inference
Estimate FPGA resource requirements (DSP, memory, logic)
Choose appropriate Gowin FPGA based on requirements
Implement inference engine optimized for target model
Validate accuracy and performance on hardware
Optimize based on profiling results
decisionFramework.decisionTree: If accuracy requirement > 95%, consider ResNet-18 or larger. If latency requirement < 20ms, verify DSP resources are sufficient. If power budget < 3W, consider GW2A-18 instead of GW2A-55. If model size > 50MB, ensure sufficient DDR3 bandwidth.

keyMetrics.accuracyRetention: <1% loss with INT8 quantization for most models

keyTakeaways: Design models specifically for edge deployment, don't just port cloud models
Quantization to INT8 is essential for efficient FPGA implementation
Memory bandwidth is often the bottleneck, not compute
Start with reference designs and benchmark before custom development
Consider model update mechanisms for field deployment
commonPitfalls: Over-estimating FPGA inference performance without benchmarking
Under-estimating memory requirements for model weights
Neglecting quantization impact on model accuracy
Ignoring thermal constraints in compact enclosures
bestPractices: Use DSP blocks efficiently by matching data widths to native precision
Implement ping-pong buffers for continuous data flow
Profile memory access patterns to optimize DDR efficiency
Design modular accelerators that can be reused across projects

faqs.question: What neural network architectures are supported by this accelerator?
faqs.answer: The accelerator supports a variety of CNN architectures commonly used for edge applications. Standard supported networks include: MobileNetV1/V2 (optimized for mobile and edge), ResNet-18/34 (good accuracy-efficiency tradeoff), SqueezeNet (very compact), and custom architectures designed for specific applications. The FPGA-based implementation provides flexibility to support novel architectures as they emerge. For a specific network, we can analyze the layer types and dimensions to determine compatibility and expected performance. Unsupported layers can often be implemented in the FPGA fabric or handled by the host processor. We provide tools to convert trained models from TensorFlow or PyTorch to the format required by our accelerator. Contact our FAE team with your specific model requirements for compatibility analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: Share your model architecture or requirements with our FAE team for compatibility assessment and performance estimation. We can help optimize your model for FPGA deployment.

faqs.question: How do I convert my trained model for deployment on this accelerator?
faqs.answer: Model deployment involves several steps: 1) Quantization - convert weights and activations from FP32 to INT8 using calibration dataset. We provide quantization tools that minimize accuracy loss. 2) Model conversion - translate the quantized model to our internal format using the provided compiler. 3) Optimization - the compiler applies hardware-specific optimizations including layer fusion and memory layout optimization. 4) Validation - run inference on test dataset to verify accuracy meets requirements. 5) Deployment - load the compiled model onto the target hardware via USB or flash programming. The process typically takes 1-2 days once the workflow is established. We provide example models and tutorials to help you get started. For complex models, our FAE team can assist with optimization to achieve target performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with our model conversion tutorial using one of the example models. Contact us for support with your specific model conversion and optimization.

faqs.question: What accuracy can I expect after quantization to INT8?
faqs.answer: INT8 quantization typically results in minimal accuracy loss for most CNNs. In our experience: Classification tasks (ImageNet) - typically <1% top-1 accuracy loss compared to FP32. Object detection (COCO) - mAP reduction of 0.5-2% depending on model. The actual impact depends on the model architecture and the calibration dataset quality. We recommend using a representative calibration dataset (100-1000 images) that covers the expected input distribution. Some layers are more sensitive to quantization - we provide options for mixed precision where critical layers can use higher precision. For applications requiring maximum accuracy, we can implement FP16 or hybrid precision at the cost of reduced performance. We provide accuracy benchmarking tools to evaluate quantization impact before deployment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Evaluate accuracy on your specific application dataset after quantization. Contact us for quantization optimization if accuracy loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.

faqs.question: Can I update the neural network model in the field?
faqs.answer: Yes, the system supports field model updates through multiple mechanisms: 1) USB interface - connect to PC and upload new model via configuration software. 2) Over-the-air update - if the system has network connectivity, models can be updated remotely. 3) SD card - load new model from removable storage. 4) Host processor - embedded system can update FPGA model via SPI or other interface. Model updates typically take 10-30 seconds depending on model size. The system can store multiple models and switch between them. We recommend implementing version management and rollback capability for production deployments. Security features including model encryption and signature verification can be implemented to protect intellectual property. The FPGA's reconfigurability makes it ideal for applications requiring model evolution over time.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design your update mechanism based on system connectivity and security requirements. Contact us for secure update implementation guidance.

faqs.question: How does this compare to GPU or dedicated AI accelerator solutions?
faqs.answer: FPGA-based AI acceleration offers distinct trade-offs compared to alternatives: Compared to GPUs, FPGAs provide lower power consumption, deterministic latency, and better efficiency for small batch inference typical of edge applications. However, GPUs offer easier programming and better performance for large models. Compared to dedicated AI accelerators (like Google Coral or Intel Movidius), FPGAs offer greater flexibility to support custom layers and evolving model architectures, but may require more development effort. The Gowin FPGA solution specifically offers competitive performance-per-watt and performance-per-dollar, with the added benefit of being able to integrate custom pre/post-processing logic alongside the AI engine. For applications requiring <10W power consumption, deterministic latency, and flexibility to customize the hardware, FPGA is often the optimal choice.
faqs.decisionGuide: Evaluate your requirements for power, latency determinism, model flexibility, and development effort. Contact us for a detailed comparison based on your specific use case.

solutions.title: Edge AI Vision Solution
solutions.subtitle: Real-time AI inference on FPGA for industrial inspection and smart cameras
solutions.description: Complete edge AI solution combining Gowin FPGAs with optimized neural network accelerators for real-time image processing and object detection.
solutions.longDescription:
The Edge AI Vision Solution from Gowin enables real-time artificial intelligence inference directly on FPGA hardware, eliminating the need for cloud connectivity or external AI processors. This solution is ideal for industrial quality inspection, smart surveillance cameras, and autonomous systems requiring immediate decision-making.

The solution leverages Gowin's Arora FPGA family with integrated DSP blocks and high-speed memory interfaces to implement efficient neural network accelerators. Pre-optimized IP cores support popular CNN architectures including MobileNet, ResNet, and custom models trained for specific applications.

Key capabilities include: real-time object detection at 30+ FPS, classification accuracy comparable to GPU solutions, low latency response for time-critical applications, and power consumption under 5W for edge deployment. The solution supports both monochrome and color camera inputs with resolutions up to 4K.

Implementation includes reference designs for industrial camera interfaces (GigE Vision, USB3 Vision), pre-trained models for common inspection tasks, and tools for converting TensorFlow/PyTorch models to FPGA-optimized implementations. BeiLuo's FAE team provides comprehensive support for model optimization and system integration.

products.role: Neural network accelerator and image processing

customerCases.challenge: Needed real-time defect detection on production line with 99%+ accuracy and <100ms latency.
customerCases.solution: Deployed Edge AI Vision Solution with GW2A-LV55 implementing custom CNN for solder joint inspection.
customerCases.result: Eliminated manual inspection, reduced defect escape rate by 95%, increased throughput by 30%.

customerCases.challenge: Required edge-based people counting and occupancy monitoring for privacy compliance.
customerCases.solution: Implemented distributed AI cameras using GW2A-LV18 with optimized MobileNet for person detection.
customerCases.result: Deployed 500+ cameras across city infrastructure with centralized monitoring dashboard.

faeInsights.insight: Edge AI on FPGA offers unique advantages for industrial applications: deterministic latency, long-term availability, and the ability to update models without hardware changes. The key is proper model optimization - a well-quantized INT8 model can achieve 95%+ of floating-point accuracy with 10x efficiency improvement.
faeInsights.logic: Solution architecture: Camera input → Preprocessing (GW1N) → AI inference (GW2A) → Decision output. Pipeline optimized for minimal latency with parallel processing.
keyTakeaways: Model quantization is critical for FPGA efficiency
FPGA offers deterministic timing vs GPUs
bestPractices: Quantize models to INT8 during training
Use DDR3 for large model storage
faeInsights.content: Based on extensive deployment experience, this Edge AI Vision Solution addresses the key challenges of real-time inference: latency, power, and accuracy. The FPGA-based approach provides unique advantages for industrial applications.

decisionFramework.title: Decision Framework for Edge AI
steps: Define accuracy and latency requirements
Select appropriate CNN architecture
Quantize and optimize model
Prototype on development board
Validate in target environment

faqs.question: What neural network architectures are supported?
faqs.answer: The Edge AI Vision Solution supports major CNN architectures including MobileNet V1/V2/V3 (optimized for edge), ResNet-18/34/50, SSD for object detection, YOLO-Tiny for real-time detection, and custom architectures. Gowin provides pre-optimized IP cores for these networks. For custom models, the Gowin AI Compiler converts TensorFlow/PyTorch models to FPGA implementations. Supported layers: Conv2D, DepthwiseConv, Fully Connected, ReLU, BatchNorm, MaxPool, AvgPool, Softmax. Contact FAE for specific architecture support.
faqs.decisionGuide: MobileNet for efficiency, ResNet for accuracy, SSD/YOLO for detection. Contact FAE for architecture selection guidance.

faqs.question: What frame rates and resolutions are achievable?
faqs.answer: Performance depends on FPGA device and model complexity: GW2A-LV18: 720p@60 FPS with MobileNet, 1080p@30 FPS with optimized models; GW2A-LV55: 1080p@60 FPS or 4K@30 FPS with larger models. Object detection (SSD) typically achieves 30 FPS at 720p. Latency ranges from 16ms (single frame) to 33ms (pipeline). For multi-camera applications, the solution can process 4x 720p streams simultaneously on GW2A-LV55. Contact FAE for performance estimation based on your specific model.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select FPGA based on resolution and frame rate requirements. LV18 for 720p, LV55 for 1080p/4K.
